---
title: "JAX and XLA: levels of representation"
---

The [previous chapter](hardware.qmd) ended on a promise: most of `nitrix` is plain
JAX, yet it runs fast on the GPU because the **XLA compiler** rewrites memory-bound
chains of operations into single kernels that read once and write once — climbing
the roofline without a line of CUDA. This chapter makes good on that promise, and
in doing so introduces the idea the whole unit turns on. A `nitrix` function is a
**pure function**; tracing it produces a small program-as-data called a `jaxpr`;
and *compiling*, *batching*, and *differentiating* are all **reinterpretations of
that one trace** ([the index's first thread](index.qmd#sec-thread-trace)). `jit`
and `grad` are not separate features bolted onto a numerics library — they are the
same move.

We build it in order: what a pure function and its **trace** are; the **ladder of
representations** a program descends from Python to GPU assembly; how XLA wins
through **operator fusion**; the transformations `jit`/`vmap`/`grad` as
reinterpretations of the trace; the price the approach charges in **control flow**;
and what all of it means for how `nitrix` is written.

## Pure functions and the trace {#sec-trace}

A **pure function** is one whose output depends only on its inputs, and which
changes nothing else in the world — no global state mutated, no file written, no
hidden counter advanced. Given the same arguments it always returns the same value.
`nitrix` holds to this strictly: a public symbol takes arrays (or PyTrees of
arrays, perhaps a random key or a static configuration) and returns arrays, with no
module objects carrying hidden state. That discipline can feel austere, but it buys
the entire chapter, because a pure function can be **traced**.

Tracing means running the function not on real data but on **abstract stand-ins** —
*tracer* objects that carry only a shape and a dtype, not values. As the function
executes, every primitive operation it calls on a tracer is *recorded* rather than
performed. The result is a transcript of exactly which primitives ran, in which
order, on which intermediates: a typed, functional little program called a
**`jaxpr`** (JAX expression). For example, the function

```python
import jax, jax.numpy as jnp

def f(x, y):
    return jnp.sum(jnp.tanh(x @ y))

jax.make_jaxpr(f)(jnp.ones((3, 3)), jnp.ones((3, 3)))
```

traces to a `jaxpr` whose body is a short sequence of primitives — a `dot_general`
(the matrix multiply), a `tanh`, and a `reduce_sum` — with explicit typed inputs
and outputs:

```text
{ lambda ; a:f32[3,3] b:f32[3,3]. let
    c:f32[3,3] = dot_general[...] a b
    d:f32[3,3] = tanh c
    e:f32[] = reduce_sum[axes=(0,1)] d
  in (e,) }
```

This is the pivot of the whole approach. The program is now a **data structure**,
not a black-box Python callable, and a data structure can be inspected, rewritten,
and re-interpreted. Everything below — compilation, batching, differentiation — is
a transformation of this object. (Note the cost of admission, which we return to in
[the control-flow section](#sec-control-flow): the trace records the operations
that *actually ran* for the given shapes, so it is **static** — it cannot contain a
branch whose direction depends on a traced *value*.)

## The ladder of representations {#sec-ladder}

A `jaxpr` is still far above the metal. Getting from it to instructions a GPU
executes is a descent down a **ladder of representations**, each lower rung more
explicit and closer to hardware, each transition a *lowering* performed by a
compiler stage (@fig-ladder). The high rungs are portable and readable; the low
rungs are fast and device-specific; the compiler's job is to bridge them.

::: {#fig-ladder}
<svg viewBox="0 0 720 400" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="A program descends a ladder of representations: pure Python function, then jaxpr, then HLO, then optimised HLO, then GPU PTX or CPU LLVM, with each transition a compiler stage."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <defs>
    <marker id="lah" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#55606b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <rect x="200" y="20"  width="320" height="48" rx="6" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="360" y="49" text-anchor="middle">pure Python function</text>
  <rect x="200" y="98" width="320" height="48" rx="6" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="360" y="121" text-anchor="middle"><tspan font-family="monospace">jaxpr</tspan> — JAX primitives</text>
  <text x="360" y="138" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">functional, typed, device-agnostic</text>
  <rect x="200" y="176" width="320" height="48" rx="6" fill="#fbf0d6" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="360" y="199" text-anchor="middle">StableHLO / HLO</text>
  <text x="360" y="216" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">XLA's linear-algebra IR</text>
  <rect x="200" y="254" width="320" height="48" rx="6" fill="#fbf0d6" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="360" y="277" text-anchor="middle">optimised HLO</text>
  <text x="360" y="294" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">fused, laid out, buffers assigned</text>
  <rect x="200" y="332" width="320" height="48" rx="6" fill="#e8ddec" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="360" y="355" text-anchor="middle">PTX (GPU) · LLVM IR (CPU)</text>
  <text x="360" y="372" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">device machine code</text>
  <line x1="360" y1="68"  x2="360" y2="96"  stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#lah)"/>
  <line x1="360" y1="146" x2="360" y2="174" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#lah)"/>
  <line x1="360" y1="224" x2="360" y2="252" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#lah)"/>
  <line x1="360" y1="302" x2="360" y2="330" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#lah)"/>
  <text x="540" y="87"  font-size="11" font-style="italic" fill="#444b53">trace</text>
  <text x="540" y="165" font-size="11" font-style="italic" fill="#444b53">lower</text>
  <text x="540" y="243" font-size="11" font-style="italic" fill="#444b53">XLA optimise</text>
  <text x="540" y="321" font-size="11" font-style="italic" fill="#444b53">codegen (LLVM)</text>
  <text x="150" y="49"  text-anchor="end" font-size="11" fill="#888">portable,</text>
  <text x="150" y="63"  text-anchor="end" font-size="11" fill="#888">readable</text>
  <text x="150" y="356" text-anchor="end" font-size="11" fill="#888">fast,</text>
  <text x="150" y="370" text-anchor="end" font-size="11" fill="#888">device-specific</text>
</svg>

The ladder of representations. A pure function is *traced* to a `jaxpr`, *lowered*
to XLA's HLO, *optimised* by XLA (the rung where fusion happens), and finally turned
into device machine code — PTX for an NVIDIA GPU, LLVM IR for a CPU. High rungs are
portable; low rungs are fast; the compiler bridges them.
:::

The acronym is literal: **XLA** is *Accelerated Linear Algebra*, a compiler whose
input is a graph of array operations (HLO — *high-level operations*) and whose
output is optimised machine code for a target backend. JAX lowers a `jaxpr` to HLO,
hands it to XLA, and caches the compiled result. You never write HLO; you write
Python, and the ladder is descended for you on the first call.

## How XLA wins: operator fusion {#sec-fusion}

The single most important thing XLA does — and the reason the roofline chapter was
a prerequisite — is **operator fusion**. Recall the governing fact of GPU
performance: arithmetic is cheap, moving data is expensive, and a chain of simple
operations is **memory-bound** (it sits on the sloped roof). Consider a typical such
chain, say `z = tanh(a * x + b)`: a multiply, an add, a `tanh`, each elementwise.

Run unfused, each operation is its own kernel: it reads its input array from global
memory (HBM), does one cheap arithmetic step per element, and writes the result
*back* to HBM for the next operation to read. Three operations, three round-trips to
the slowest memory on the device — and almost all the time is that traffic, not the
arithmetic. **Fused**, XLA emits *one* kernel that reads the input once, keeps each
element in a register while it runs the multiply, the add, and the `tanh` in
sequence, and writes the result once. Same arithmetic; a fraction of the memory
traffic. @fig-fusion contrasts the two.

::: {#fig-fusion}
<svg viewBox="0 0 760 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="Unfused, three elementwise operations each round-trip through HBM; fused, one kernel reads the input once, runs all three operations in registers, and writes once."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <defs>
    <marker id="fah" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#9aa3ab"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <!-- unfused -->
  <text x="40" y="30" font-weight="bold">unfused: three kernels, three HBM round-trips</text>
  <rect x="40" y="44" width="680" height="30" rx="4" fill="#f3dede" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="380" y="64" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">HBM (global memory)</text>
  <g fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d">
    <rect x="120" y="108" width="120" height="40" rx="5"/>
    <rect x="320" y="108" width="120" height="40" rx="5"/>
    <rect x="520" y="108" width="120" height="40" rx="5"/>
  </g>
  <text x="180" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">multiply</text>
  <text x="380" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">add</text>
  <text x="580" y="133" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">tanh</text>
  <g stroke="#9aa3ab" stroke-width="1.2">
    <line x1="150" y1="74" x2="150" y2="106" marker-end="url(#fah)"/>
    <line x1="210" y1="106" x2="210" y2="76" marker-end="url(#fah)"/>
    <line x1="350" y1="74" x2="350" y2="106" marker-end="url(#fah)"/>
    <line x1="410" y1="106" x2="410" y2="76" marker-end="url(#fah)"/>
    <line x1="550" y1="74" x2="550" y2="106" marker-end="url(#fah)"/>
    <line x1="610" y1="106" x2="610" y2="76" marker-end="url(#fah)"/>
  </g>
  <text x="380" y="166" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#b03b4b">6 HBM transfers (read+write per op)</text>
  <!-- fused -->
  <text x="40" y="216" font-weight="bold">fused: one kernel, one read and one write</text>
  <rect x="40" y="230" width="680" height="30" rx="4" fill="#f3dede" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="380" y="250" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">HBM (global memory)</text>
  <rect x="220" y="280" width="320" height="34" rx="5" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="380" y="302" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">multiply → add → tanh  (in registers)</text>
  <g stroke="#9aa3ab" stroke-width="1.2">
    <line x1="300" y1="260" x2="300" y2="278" marker-end="url(#fah)"/>
    <line x1="460" y1="278" x2="460" y2="262" marker-end="url(#fah)"/>
  </g>
  <text x="380" y="280" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#2c6a45">2 HBM transfers</text>
</svg>

Operator fusion. Three elementwise operations run unfused (top) each read their
input from HBM and write their output back — six transfers across the slowest
memory. Fused (bottom), one kernel reads once, runs all three in registers, and
writes once — two transfers. For memory-bound work, the traffic *is* the cost, so
this is most of the speed-up.
:::

The win grows with the length of the chain, because the unfused traffic scales with
the number of operations while the fused traffic stays fixed at one read and one
write. @fig-fusion-traffic makes that quantitative.

```{python}
#| label: fig-fusion-traffic
#| fig-cap: "HBM traffic for a chain of n elementwise operations on one array, in units of the array size. Unfused, each operation reads and writes the array, so traffic grows as 2n; fused into a single kernel, the data is read once and written once regardless of chain length. Because memory-bound work is timed by bytes moved, the fused curve is also (roughly) the run-time curve."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

n = np.arange(1, 9)
unfused = 2 * n          # read + write per op, in units of one array
fused = np.full_like(n, 2)   # one read + one write, total

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 3.8))
ax.plot(n, unfused, "-o", color="#b03b4b", lw=2, label="unfused  (2n)")
ax.plot(n, fused, "-o", color="#2c6a45", lw=2, label="fused  (2)")
ax.fill_between(n, fused, unfused, color="#b03b4b", alpha=0.08)
ax.set_xlabel("operations in the elementwise chain  (n)")
ax.set_ylabel("HBM traffic  (× array size)")
ax.set_title("Fusion turns 2n memory round-trips into 2")
ax.legend()
ax.set_ylim(0, 17)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Fusion is the headline, but XLA does more on the "optimise" rung: it assigns memory
**layouts** so tensors are stored in the order kernels want to read them; it
**reuses buffers** (and, with *donation*, overwrites an input that will not be
needed again, so a large array can be updated in place); it folds constants and
schedules independent work concurrently. All of it is invisible from the Python
source — you write array operations at the top of the ladder, and the compiler
spends its effort at the bottom.

## The transformations: `jit`, `vmap`, `grad` {#sec-transforms}

Here is where the chapter's thesis becomes concrete. Because the program is a
traced data structure, JAX offers **function transformations** — each takes a
function and returns a new function, by reinterpreting the trace. Three matter, and
they *compose*.

**`jit` — compile.** `jax.jit(f)` traces `f`, lowers the `jaxpr` down the ladder,
and hands it to XLA; the compiled kernel is **cached**, keyed on the input shapes
and dtypes (and any values marked static). The first call pays the compile; every
later call at the same shapes runs the optimised, fused kernel. This is the
"compile" arrow of [the index's diagram](index.qmd#sec-trace).

```python
fast = jax.jit(f)        # trace + lower + XLA-compile on first call, then cache
fast(x, y)               # subsequent calls reuse the compiled, fused kernel
```

**`vmap` — batch.** `jax.vmap(f)` adds a batch dimension to every primitive in the
trace, turning a function written for *one* example into one that runs over a whole
batch — with no Python loop and no rewrite of `f`. A primitive's *batching rule*
says how it gains an axis (@fig-vmap); the transformation threads that axis through
the entire `jaxpr`. This is exactly how `nitrix` is **mass-univariate**: write the
model for one voxel, `vmap` it across $10^5$–$10^6$ voxels, and the batched program
compiles to one kernel.

```python
batched = jax.vmap(f)    # f written for one example...
batched(xs, ys)          # ...now maps over a leading batch axis, no loop
```

::: {#fig-vmap}
<svg viewBox="0 0 700 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="vmap lifts a function on a single example to a function on a batch by adding a leading batch axis to every array."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <defs>
    <marker id="vah" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#55606b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <rect x="60" y="80" width="40" height="40" rx="4" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="80" y="105" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">x</text>
  <rect x="150" y="80" width="90" height="40" rx="5" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="195" y="105" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">f</text>
  <rect x="290" y="80" width="40" height="40" rx="4" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="310" y="105" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">y</text>
  <line x1="100" y1="100" x2="148" y2="100" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#vah)"/>
  <line x1="240" y1="100" x2="288" y2="100" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#vah)"/>
  <text x="195" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">one example</text>
  <text x="430" y="105" text-anchor="middle" font-size="22" fill="#55606b">⇒</text>
  <g fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d">
    <rect x="500" y="64" width="36" height="36" rx="4"/><rect x="506" y="72" width="36" height="36" rx="4"/><rect x="512" y="80" width="36" height="36" rx="4"/>
  </g>
  <rect x="566" y="72" width="80" height="40" rx="5" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="606" y="97" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">vmap(f)</text>
  <text x="573" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">a batch (+1 axis)</text>
</svg>

`vmap` lifts a function written for a single example to one that runs over a whole
batch, by adding a leading batch axis to every array and threading it through every
primitive. No loop is written; the batched program compiles to one kernel.
:::

**`grad` — differentiate.** `jax.grad(f)` (and the lower-level `jvp`/`vjp`)
reinterpret the trace to compute derivatives — linearising the program and, for
reverse mode, transposing that linear map. This is the "linearise" and "transpose"
arrows of the diagram, and it is the whole subject of the
[next chapter](differentiation.qmd); here we only place it alongside its siblings.

The point is that all three are the *same kind of object* — a transformation of one
trace — so they **compose** freely. The idiom at the heart of large-scale
differentiable numerics,

```python
jax.jit(jax.vmap(jax.grad(loss)))   # compiled, batched, differentiated — one trace
```

reads right-to-left as: differentiate the per-example loss, batch it over the
dataset, compile the whole thing. One traced program, three reinterpretations,
stacked.

## The price of a static graph: control flow {#sec-control-flow}

The power of the trace comes from its being **static** — a fixed graph of
operations on arrays of known shape — and that is also its one real constraint. The
trace records the operations that ran for the *abstract* inputs, so it cannot
contain a decision that depends on a traced **value**. A Python `if x > 0:` on a
traced `x` has no answer at trace time (there is no value, only a shape), and a
Python `while` whose iteration count depends on the data would bake in whatever
count the trace happened to see. Two consequences shape how `nitrix` is written.

**Data-dependent control flow becomes a primitive.** Instead of Python branches and
loops over traced values, JAX provides *structured* control-flow primitives that
live inside the trace: `lax.cond` (choose a branch by a traced predicate),
`lax.while_loop` (loop until a traced condition), `lax.scan` and `lax.fori_loop`
(a fixed number of iterations, compiled *once* rather than unrolled). These are why
the statistics unit's solvers run a **fixed iteration budget** wrapped in a
`lax.scan` — a branch-free, fixed-length loop is what stays `vmap`-clean and
compiles to one kernel — and why the registration unit rolls its optimiser and its
scaling-and-squaring with `lax.scan` rather than Python loops. It is the
software-level echo of the hardware's preference for uniform, non-divergent control
flow from the [last chapter](hardware.qmd#sec-simt).

**Shapes are part of the cache key.** Because the compiled kernel is specialised to
the shapes it was traced with, calling a `jit`-ed function with a *new* shape forces
a re-trace and re-compile. Stable shapes mean you pay the compile once; shapes that
drift on every call mean you pay it every call. This is why values that must be
known at trace time — a number of basis functions, an interpolation kernel's
support, a model's structural configuration — are passed as **static** arguments or
carried in frozen, hashable configuration records that ride `jit` as static
operands (the `RegistrationSpec` and `Family` records you meet in the registration
and statistics units), rather than as traced arrays.

## What this means for `nitrix` {#sec-nitrix}

Step back and the library's design reads straight off this chapter.

- **Purity is the enabling constraint.** Because every public symbol is a pure
  function over arrays, every one is traceable — and therefore `jit`-able,
  `vmap`-able, and differentiable, all by the same mechanism. The "no module
  objects, no hidden state" rule from the [index](index.qmd#sec-library) is not
  fastidiousness; it is what makes the transformations apply at all.
- **"Just JAX" is fast because of fusion.** The bulk of `nitrix` is plain JAX
  precisely because XLA fuses its memory-bound reductions and elementwise chains
  into single kernels — the [hardware chapter](hardware.qmd#sec-roofline)'s
  roofline win, obtained by the compiler. Hand-written Pallas kernels appear only
  where fusion cannot capture enough reuse (the **backend** axis), and even then
  beside a pure-JAX reference.
- **The transformations compose across the whole library.** Mass-univariate fitting
  is `vmap` over voxels; a differentiable registration layer is `grad` through a
  warp; running either at scale is `jit` over the composition. The same three
  reinterpretations recur on every page of the domain units.
- **Static-shape discipline is everywhere.** Fixed iteration counts, frozen
  configuration records as static arguments, structured `lax` control flow — these
  are not quirks but the direct consequence of compiling a static trace, and they
  are why a fit `vmap`-ed across a whole brain compiles once and runs as one kernel.
  (The same staticness is what lets `nitrix` pin every platform-dependent numerical
  choice to a reproducible default under `NITRIX_REPRODUCIBLE`, so a traced program
  gives bit-stable results across machines.)

The thread tying it together is [the index's first one](index.qmd#sec-thread-trace):
**one trace, many interpretations.** Compilation made the program fast;
differentiation will make it *learn*. They are the same skill — reinterpreting a
trace — and the [next chapter](differentiation.qmd) takes up the second half.

## Further reading

- Bradbury, J. et al. (2018). *JAX: composable transformations of Python+NumPy
  programs.* — the framework and its design around traceable pure functions; see
  also the [JAX documentation](https://docs.jax.dev) on `jit`, `vmap`, `grad`, and
  `jaxpr`s.
- Frostig, R., Johnson, M. J. & Leary, C. (2018). *Compiling machine learning
  programs via high-level tracing.* MLSys. — tracing to a typed IR and lowering it
  to XLA.
- The [XLA documentation](https://openxla.org/xla) on HLO, operator fusion, and
  layout assignment — the optimisations this chapter attributes to the "optimise"
  rung of the ladder.
- The JAX *Control flow and logical operators with JIT* and *Common gotchas* guides
  — the practical face of the static-trace constraint (`lax.cond`/`scan`/
  `while_loop`, and shape-driven recompilation).

## See also

- The purity boundary and dispatch axes:
  [`nitrix`'s architecture](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix) (`SPEC.md`), and
  the backend resolver
  [`_internal/backend.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/_internal/backend.py).
- Reproducibility under a static trace: the divergent-operations registry
  [`_internal/_divergent_ops.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/_internal/_divergent_ops.py)
  and `nitrix.reproducible()`.
- The static-shape discipline in practice: the statistics unit's
  [fixed-iteration solvers](../statistics/solvers.qmd) and the registration unit's
  [`lax.scan`-rolled optimisers](../registration/performance.qmd).
- Previous chapter: [Programming the GPU](hardware.qmd) — the machine XLA compiles
  for. Next chapter: [Differentiation as linear algebra](differentiation.qmd) — the
  "linearise" and "transpose" reinterpretations of the trace.
